narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 31 (well under the average 36.70), and low decile rankings in your school's Year 1 and Year 3 grades—and calculated a 91.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected; law schools use this prediction to prioritize admits likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three biggest factors pushing the model toward this "fail" prediction, in order of influence, were your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1 (far below the average 5.53), your Year 1 decile of 1 (versus the typical 5.71), and your family's upper-middle income bracket. Your low Year 3 decile had the strongest pull toward failure, as it signals consistently weak performance relative to peers in a key later academic year that strongly correlates with bar success. Similarly, your bottom-decile Year 1 grades reinforced this concern, showing early struggles that the model sees as a red flag for law school rigor. Even your family's upper-middle income, while not a massive driver, nudged the prediction slightly toward failure compared to lower-income backgrounds that sometimes predict better bar outcomes in the data. Overall, your academic metrics placed you below typical successful applicants, outweighing positives like planning full-time study (the norm for 92.7% of students). This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or your dedication—many factors like personal growth aren't captured here—but a data-driven risk assessment. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your undergrad record through a post-bac program or additional coursework to improve those decile rankings, and gathering strong letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value comebacks. You're capable of turning this around with targeted effort.
